critic_raw:
This is a carefully designed experiment with genuine attention to leakage, evaluation, and confound control. I'll work through the real issues I can find.

**Issue 1: Account-level grouping creates a fundamental tension with the split strategy**

The methodology states that 'all tickets from a customer appear in only one split.' But the sequence feature for any given test ticket is built from that account's *prior* tickets — which, if the account's tickets are all in the test set, must come from the test window itself or from some external source. If an account's earliest ticket is in Month 25, their sequence features may reference tickets that are also in the test set but earlier in Month 25. This is not standard leakage (the current ticket is excluded from its own sequence), but the sequence features for test-set tickets from accounts that only appear in the test window are either (a) empty (violating the 90-day history requirement, which should filter them out) or (b) drawn from other test-set tickets from the same account. The 90-day pre-index history requirement mitigates this significantly — accounts must have activity before their index ticket — but the requirement is framed relative to the ticket's timestamp, not relative to the train/test boundary. An account active from Month 23 onward would have their Month 25 ticket's sequence drawn from Month 23–24 tickets, which are in the validation set. The preprocessing note says aggregations use 'only tickets in the training window,' but sequence features (individual prior tickets, not aggregations) are not explicitly governed by this constraint. This is a subtle but real ambiguity: is the sequence for a test ticket built from all chronologically prior tickets (including validation-window tickets), or only from training-window tickets? The methodology does not resolve this.

Evidence: Compare model performance when sequence features for test tickets are (a) built from all prior tickets vs. (b) built from training-window tickets only. If (a) outperforms (b), there is effective leakage through the sequence window.

**Issue 2: The 90-day account history filter creates a survivorship selection bias**

Restricting to accounts with ≥90 days of prior support activity before the index ticket systematically excludes new customers and accounts with infrequent but critical issues. Enterprise accounts tend to have long histories; SMB accounts tend to churn faster or have sparse histories. This selection filter will disproportionately enrich the evaluation corpus with enterprise accounts and high-volume customers — precisely the segment cited as a target of interest. The stated goal of 'testing on stable interaction patterns rather than partially observed onboarding behavior' is reasonable, but it means the experiment measures performance on a population that is already self-selected for having the interaction patterns the sequence model needs to work. When the model is deployed in production, it will encounter tickets from accounts that do not meet this threshold. The reported macro-F1 will not generalize to that population.

The experiment does stratify by prior ticket availability (0–5, 6–20, 20+), but this stratification is *after* the 90-day filter is applied — so the '0–5 prior tickets' stratum still only contains accounts with ≥90 days of recorded activity but few tickets in that window, not true new accounts. The gap between evaluated and deployed population is not acknowledged.

Evidence: Report performance on accounts that fail the 90-day filter (using a text-only evaluation, since sequence features are undefined). If that excluded population is large and has substantially different category distributions, the deployment gap is real.

**Issue 3: The baseline is not a fair attributional test for the sequence modeling hypothesis**

The methodology claims 'the difference in performance can be cleanly attributed to the sequence modeling component' because the baseline receives equivalent tuning. But the proposed model has two architectural advantages over the baseline simultaneously: (1) a deep contextual text encoder (DistilBERT) vs. TF-IDF, and (2) sequence modeling. If the proposed model outperforms the baseline, it is impossible to attribute the gain to sequence modeling rather than to the superior text representation. This is a standard ablation gap: the missing baseline is a DistilBERT text-only model without sequence features. Without it, any observed gain confounds text encoder quality with sequence modeling effectiveness.

The methodology explicitly states that causality about *why* sequences help is out of scope and would require ablation studies. But the primary scientific claim — that sequence modeling improves performance — requires this ablation to be credible. Observing that the full model beats TF-IDF + logistic regression establishes only that the full system is better, not that sequences are responsible.

Evidence: Add a third model: DistilBERT pooled representation → dense classification head, no sequence branch, trained identically. If this model matches or exceeds the dual-branch model, sequences contribute nothing. This baseline is cheap to implement and is a prerequisite for the stated hypothesis.

**Issue 4: Macro-F1 on rare categories is vulnerable to instability from small support sets**

The methodology explicitly targets rare categories and uses macro-F1 to weight them equally. But categories with <0.1% frequency are excluded as likely labeling errors. The remaining rare categories (say, 0.1%–2% frequency) will have very few test examples — potentially tens of examples across a 12-month test window with millions of tickets. Macro-F1 computed over such categories is highly sensitive to a small number of correct or incorrect predictions. A single category where the model predicts 3 correct out of 5 vs. 2 correct out of 5 can swing macro-F1 noticeably. The experiment does not report confidence intervals or per-category support counts, so reported macro-F1 improvements on rare categories may not be statistically distinguishable from noise.

Evidence: Report 95% bootstrap confidence intervals on macro-F1, stratified by rare vs. common categories. If the confidence intervals overlap between the baseline and proposed model for the rare-category stratum, the claimed benefit on rare classes is not established.

**Summary of genuine issues:**
1. Sequence feature construction for test tickets is ambiguous regarding whether validation-window tickets are used, creating potential leakage.
2. The 90-day history filter creates a selection bias that limits generalization to the deployed population.
3. The baseline confounds text encoder quality with sequence modeling; the experiment cannot attribute gains to sequences without a DistilBERT text-only ablation.
4. Macro-F1 on rare categories is statistically fragile without confidence intervals given small per-category support.

Issue 3 is the most fundamental: it undermines the central scientific claim of the experiment. Issues 1 and 2 are real but partially mitigated by the design. Issue 4 is a reporting gap that affects interpretability of the main result.
all_issues_raised: Sequence feature construction for test tickets is ambiguous: the methodology does not specify whether prior sequences are built from training-window tickets only or all chronologically prior tickets, potentially including validation-set tickets.

The 90-day account history filter introduces survivorship selection bias, enriching the evaluation corpus with enterprise and high-volume accounts and creating a deployment gap for accounts that don't meet the filter threshold.

The baseline confounds TF-IDF vs. DistilBERT text encoding with the presence vs. absence of sequence modeling; without a DistilBERT text-only ablation, gains cannot be attributed to the sequence component.
Macro-F1 on rare categories is statistically fragile due to small per-category test set support, and the methodology does not report confidence intervals to distinguish real gains from noise.
all_issues_adjudicated: Sequence feature construction for test tickets is ambiguous: the methodology does not specify whether prior sequences are built from training-window tickets only or all chronologically prior tickets, potentially including validation-set tickets.

The 90-day account history filter introduces survivorship selection bias, enriching the evaluation corpus with enterprise and high-volume accounts and creating a deployment gap for accounts that don't meet the filter threshold.

The baseline confounds TF-IDF vs. DistilBERT text encoding with the presence vs. absence of sequence modeling; without a DistilBERT text-only ablation, gains cannot be attributed to the sequence component.
Macro-F1 on rare categories is statistically fragile due to small per-category test set support, and the methodology does not report confidence intervals to distinguish real gains from noise.
